coredata.dc:title: A selective approach towards synthesis of poly (3‑bromo thiophene)/graphene quantum dot composites via in-situ and ex-situ routes: Application in light emission and photocurrent generation
coredata.dc:creator: Sardar, Soumen
Roy, Indranil
Chakraborty, Subhadeep
Ghosh, Abhisek Brata
Bandyopadhyay, Abhijit
coredata.dc:description: This article elucidates fabrication of a smart composite from poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) and graphene quantum dot (GQD) through facile in-situ and ex-situ routes and demonstrates their photo-physical and photo-electrochemical behaviors. 3-bromothiophene was first polymerized to poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) in presence of graphene oxide (GO) and then converted into PTBr/GQD composite through solvo-thermal route in the in-situ process while in the ex-situ process, both graphene oxide (GO) and poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) were first combined ex-situ and then solvo-thermally treated to fabricate the PTBr/GQD composite. Spectroscopic findings indicate safe formation of the composites however, alteration in both precursor and fabrication route had simultaneously created a demonstrable difference in interfacial interaction between poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) and graphene quantum dot (GQD) which eventually impacted their application behavior. Microscopy, Energy dispersive X-Ray (EDX) mapping and X-ray diffraction study mutually elucidated the formation of uniformly thin, fibrillar (8 nm) over-growth of amorphous poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) over graphene quantum dot (GQD) in the ex-situ composite while in the in-situ composite a comparatively non-uniform and thicker over-growth (12 nm) of poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) over graphene quantum dot (GQD) was noticed. Both the composites had shown quenched photoemission but exhibited a much improved photocurrent generation than the pristine components. However, relatively high concentration of poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) coupled with its stronger interaction with graphene quantum dot (GQD) in the in-situ composite had produced a stronger red shift and much higher photocurrent efficiency than that in the ex-situ sample.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
In-situ/ex-situ composites
Photoluminescence
J-V Characteristics

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption spectroscopic study of poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) and its composites: (a) Fourier Transform infrared and (b) Ultraviolet-Visible absorption spectra. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Appearance of suspensions of poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) and its composites in normal light and under UV A.
Morphology of in-situ and ex-situ composites (a) Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images and Energy dispersive X-Ray (EDX) spectra and (b) Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images.

Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) image analysis of the hybrid moieties: a) area and b) size (length wise) distribution of in-situ composite and (c) area and (d) size (length wise) distribution of the ex-situ composite, respectively.
Wide angle X-ray diffractogram (in powder form) of pristine poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr), pristine graphene oxide (GO), in-situ and ex-situ composites, respectively.

(a) The emission spectrum of pristine poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr), Graphene quantum dot (GQD), in-situ and ex-situ composites and (b) Fluorescence decay profiles of the in-situ and ex-situ composites-all carried out in N,N’ dimethyl formamide (DMF) at 298 K at an excitation wavelength (λ exc) of 390 nm.

Photo-electrochemical measurements with in-situ composite (a) Linear sweep voltametry in dark and under illuminated conditions (b) Time-dependent photocurrent density variation with constant light ON and OFF modes at 25 s intervals and (c) the Nyquist plot. The inset in (c) shows the equivalent circuit diagram for calculating the resistive parameters.

Photo-electrochemical measurements with ex-situ composite: (a) Linear sweep voltametry in dark and under light (b) Time-dependent photocurrent density variation with consecutive light ON and OFF modes at 25 s intervals and (c) the Nyquist plot.
Synthesis protocol of PTBr/GQD composite: (a) In-situ vs. (b) Ex-situ routes.
Summary of the photo-physical and photo-electrochemical properties of in-situ and ex-situ composites.
The resistive parameters of the composites.

head.ce:title: A selective approach towards synthesis of poly (3‑bromo thiophene)/graphene quantum dot composites via in-situ and ex-situ routes: Application in light emission and photocurrent generation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Polymer Science and Technology, University of Calcutta, 92, A.P.C. Road, Calcutta 700009, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Polymer Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Polymer Science and Technology, University of Calcutta, 92, A.P.C. Road, Calcutta 700009, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This article elucidates fabrication of a smart composite from poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) and graphene quantum dot (GQD) through facile in-situ and ex-situ routes and demonstrates their photo-physical and photo-electrochemical behaviors. 3-bromothiophene was first polymerized to poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) in presence of graphene oxide (GO) and then converted into PTBr/GQD composite through solvo-thermal route in the in-situ process while in the ex-situ process, both graphene oxide (GO) and poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) were first combined ex-situ and then solvo-thermally treated to fabricate the PTBr/GQD composite. Spectroscopic findings indicate safe formation of the composites however, alteration in both precursor and fabrication route had simultaneously created a demonstrable difference in interfacial interaction between poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) and graphene quantum dot (GQD) which eventually impacted their application behavior. Microscopy, Energy dispersive X-Ray (EDX) mapping and X-ray diffraction study mutually elucidated the formation of uniformly thin, fibrillar (8 nm) over-growth of amorphous poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) over graphene quantum dot (GQD) in the ex-situ composite while in the in-situ composite a comparatively non-uniform and thicker over-growth (12 nm) of poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) over graphene quantum dot (GQD) was noticed. Both the composites had shown quenched photoemission but exhibited a much improved photocurrent generation than the pristine components. However, relatively high concentration of poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) coupled with its stronger interaction with graphene quantum dot (GQD) in the in-situ composite had produced a stronger red shift and much higher photocurrent efficiency than that in the ex-situ sample.

ce:section.ce:para: Use of three dimensional (3D) grade nano-graphene oxide in various conducting polymers has gained impetus owing to its supreme electronic cum optoelectronic properties [1]. Three dimensional (3D) grade nano-graphene oxide or graphene quantum dot (GQD), as they are generally referred to, is a zero dimensional nanomaterial composed of a single layer of carbon atom with a few nano meter sized particle (2–50 nm) [2]. Graphene quantum dot (GQD) has superior electrical conductivity, good dispersibility, low toxicity and high luminescence property, and are suitable for unprecedented applications such as light harvester, supercapacitor, fluorescent probes etc. in optoelectronics [3,4]. The emission color and frequencies of graphene quantum dot (GQD) can precisely be optimized by varying the particle size and interaction with the surface functional groups [5]. Conducting polymers, on the other hand, are known to have excellent thermal and optical properties and are often cost effective by virtue of which they have been considered highly effective towards optoelectronic display and capacitative applications for the last few years [6]. Both conducting polymers and graphene quantum dot (GQD) could be used independently as they are highly stable in their original form [7,8] yet, the concept of formation of their composite has evolved recently to expand the application spectrum. For example, composites developed from poly (3, 4-ethylene dioxy- thiophene) or (PEDOT/Gr) [9], polyaniline/Graphene oxide [10] and polythiophene/GO [11] were used for electrocatalytic activity unlike pristine polymers and graphene quantum dot (GQD). Mombru et.al. in 2016 explored the electrical transport property of polyaniline-GQD composites for potential application in electronic device [12]. Arthisree et al. in 2017 also described similar application potential of graphene quantum dot (GQD) composite using non-conducting cellulose acetate as a host matrix [13]. Composites made of graphene quantum dot (GQD) and conducting polymer also excelled in energy application. Huang et.al. in 2014 [14] studied the enhancement of solar cell efficiency by graphene quantum dot (GQD) contained in a polymer matrix. In 2011, Gupta et.al [15] synthesized graphene quantum dot (GQD) based poly (2-‑methoxy 5-(2-ethylhexyloxy)−1,4-phenylenevinylene) composite and studied the efficiency of graphene quantum dot (GQD) as an organic light emitting diode. Such composites were predominantly synthesized in suspension via carboxylation using different reducing agents, or through electrochemical route or sometimes even through a time consuming Suzuki coupling reaction. Along with, some composites were also reportedly synthesized via a complicated potentiostatic polymerization route in presence of graphene oxide (GO), without using any additional dopants. Sriram and his co-workers fabricated poly (3- hexyl thiophene) (P3HT) deposited over graphene oxide (GO) and studied their optoelectronic properties. Presence of graphene oxide (GO) beneath poly (3- hexyl thiophene) (P3HT), significantly improved the photovoltaic performance of the device [16]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in the form of composite were also used as sensitizers in dye-sensitized solar cells [17] and as light emitting layer in organic light emitting diodes (OLEDs) [18]. Often, polymer/grapheme quantum dot (GQD) composites were synthesized starting from exfoliation of graphene with quaternary amine to graphene oxide (GO) followed by polymerization and subsequent formation of graphene quantum dot (GQD); however, the end results were not found much different from the other processes though. In view of the facts stated above, we intended to design a facile synthetic route to fabricate polymer/grapheme quantum dot (GQD) composite, assuming poly (3‑bromo thiophene) (PTBr) as a model conducting polymer, for achieving synergism in functional properties like photo-physical and photo- electrochemical behaviors. Unlike most of the reported techniques, the current methodology had engaged a few simple processes like mechanical mixing of precursors, surfactant mediated polymerization of the monomer and solvo-thermal route to synthesize the composite. The overall fabrication was accomplished vide in-situ and ex-situ routes- both were adopted concurrently to explore if the composites would develop any “dissimilar” interface impacting their end-properties. Poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) was chosen over parent Polythiophene because it is electron richer. In the in-situ route, the monomer 3-bromothiophene and graphene oxide (GO), both were used as the precursors while in the ex-situ route the already-formed poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) and graphene oxide (GO) were used as the precursors, respectively. Graphene oxide (GO) was converted into graphene quantum dot (GQD) and the composites were simultaneously developed in solvo-thermal technique. The outcome was appealing- the in-situ technique produced a composite with a stronger PTBr- GQD interface than the ex-situ one yet both of them exhibited a very similar trend in functional properties-the emission intensity was quenched whilst a synergistic effect was noticed in photocurrent generation. However, the stronger phase interaction in-situ composite had still resulted into a sharp red shift in photoemission despite the quenching effect and registered a significantly high photocurrent efficiency compared to the ex-situ one.

3-bromothiophene (monomer) with a purity level of 98% was purchased from Sigma Aldrich Chemical Company, USA. Anhydrous ferric chloride (polymerization catalyst), chloroform and N,N’-dimethyl formamide (DMF) (solvents), all of analytical grade, were procured from Loba Chemie, Mumbai, India and were used without further modification. Graphene oxide (GO) was synthesised indigenously from waste dry cell battery in the laboratory by Roy et al. [19]. Details of the synthesis and material characterization could be found in this article.

Poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) was synthesised using oxidative polymerization of 3- bromothiophene [20]. In in-situ process, 7 gm (or 0.4 mol) of anhydrous ferric chloride and 100 mg of GO were added in 15 mL chloroform in a three necked – flask under nitrogen. The solution was cooled between 0 and 4 °C in a ice bath and 1 mL of 3-bromothiophene (or 0.0106 mol), dissolved in 10 mL chloroform, was added drop wise for 10 min. The solution was continuously stirred for 6 h under similar condition and thereafter was allowed to reach the ambient temperature (27 °C) and further allowed to stand overnight. After 24 h, the mixture was filtered in a Whatmann no. 41 filter paper, the residue was collected and washed first with water and then with ethanol and then finally dried in vacuum for another 24 h. The product obtained was a black powder composed of poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) and graphene oxide (GO). The PTBr-GQD composite was prepared by solvo-thermal process. 0.025 gm of this composite was mixed with 25 mL of N,N’ dimethyl formamide (DMF) and sonicated for 30 min to form a homogeneous dispersion. It was then transferred into a metal cubette fitted with a Teflon gasket and heated for 8 h at 180 °C in an air oven with a closed lid. The product, obtained in liquid form, was collected through filtration in Whatmann no. 41 filter paper. In the ex-situ process, poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) was separately synthesised from 3-bromothiophene using oxidative polymerization technique and purified. 150 mg of poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) was dispersed into 50 mL N, N’ dimethyl formamide (DMF) and sonicated for 1 hour. 100 mg of graphene oxide (GO) was added and further sonicated for 6 hour to ensure proper PTBr–GO mixing. The dispersion was then transferred into the same metal cubette, tightly covered with the lid and heated in the air oven for 8 h at 180 °C. The liquid product was collected, filtered and dried overnight under vacuum. Both the processes are pictorially demonstrated in Scheme 1.

Microstructures of the composites and the reference sample were determined by spectroscopic analysis. Infrared spectroscopic study was carried out in a FTIR spectrophotometer purchased from Shimadzu (IR Affinity-I 8000). The samples were studied between the spectral range of 400 to 4000 cm−1 at a peak resolution of 4 cm−1. An average of 120 scans was recorded and represented for analysis. Electronic transition was studied in an Ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometer; model Lambda 25, procured from Perkin, Elmer, USA. Experiments were carried out at room temperature with the samples dispersed in N,N’ dimethyl formamide (DMF) at similar concentration. Morphology of the composites was determined using scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy and wide angle X-ray diffraction techniques. The scanning electron microscopic (SEM) images of the un-coated composites were captured (Model: ZEISS VO-MAIO) at an output potential difference of 10 KV under room temperature. Elemental composition was determined through energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis technique, associated with the microscope. The organic to inorganic mass ratio in the composites was estimated through ash content analysis carried out under nitrogen. About 2 gm of the samples were taken in a porcelain crucible and heated in a furnace previously purged with nitrogen. The organic mass completely decomposed on heating while the inorganic mass was retained. The final residue was weighed and the data was reported in the form of organic mass lost per gram of inorganic mass in the composites. The transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images of the composites were captured at an output potential difference of 200 KV (Model: JEOL JSM 2100). Imaging was carried out on air dried composites, previously solvent (N,N” dimethyl formamide, DMF) cast on carbon coated copper grid (300 mesh). The dry state crystallinity (powder form) was studied in a wide-angle X-ray diffractometer purchased from Xperto-PAN analytical, USA. CuKα, with a wavelength of 1.54 Å, was used as the X-ray source. The composites were scanned between the crystal reflection angles of 0 to 40° at a scanning speed of 1˚/min. Percent crystallinity was calculated from the diffractogram using the following standard Equation (Eq. (1)).
(1)
Crystallinity
( % )
=
I c
I a + I c
* 100
Here, Ic denotes the intensity of the crystalline peak and Ia denotes the intensity of the amorphous halo. The photo-physical property (photoluminescence or PL) was determined in N,N’ dimethyl formamide (DMF) using a Jasco Spectro Fluorometer FP-8500, Edinburg. The time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) method was employed to determine the fluorescence lifetimes using a HORIBA Jobin Yvon Fluorocube-01-NL fluorescence lifetime spectrometer. Photo-electrochemical experiments were carried out in a conventional three electrode system [reference electrode: silver/silver chloride (Ag/AgCl), counter electrode: platinum (Pt) wire, working electrode: polymer/graphene oxide on indium tin oxide (ITO)] in 0.1 M Na2SO4 [21]. The working electrode was designed by drop casting the dispersion of the PTBr/GQD composite on conducting surface of thoroughly cleaned indium tin oxide (ITO) glass, following the methodology reported by Wang et.al. [22]. The film was then dried in a hot air oven at 160 °C for 2 hour. The study was carried out using a CHI700E in linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) mode within a potential window of −1.0 V to 1.5 V (vs Reference Hydrogen Electrode, RHE) at a scan rate of 0.05 Vs−1 both under dark and illumination (300 W Xe lamp), respectively. The photoresponse of both in-situ and ex-situ composites were identified by measuring the current density chrono-amperometrically in a consecutive light on-off mode at an interval of 25 s. In order to measure the charge transfer ability of the composites, electrochemical impedance study was performed in a frequency range of 100 Hz to 105 Hz at an output potential difference of 1.5 V (vs Reference Hydrogen Electrode, RHE) with amplitude of 0.005 V using the same electrolyte used for linear sweep voltametry (LSV). The measurement was done both in light and dark modes to compare the resistive parameters for in-situ and ex-situ samples.

The infrared spectrum of pistine poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) and its composites are shown in Fig. 1 a. Transmission peaks appeared at 2892, 1635, 814 and 697 cm−1 in pristine poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) were attributed to the stretching vibrations of CH, C = C, C-S and bending vibration of C-S-C respectively. The transmission peak at 596 cm−1 denoted the stretching vibration of C-Br of poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr). In PTBr/GQD composite, additional peaks appeared at 1078, 1244, 1394, 1509 and 1661 cm−1 were due to the stretching vibrations of CO (epoxy) and COH, bending vibration of OH, skeletal vibration of benzene ring and stretching vibration of C = O of graphene quantum dot (GQD), respectively [17]. A sharp shift was noted in cases of both C-S and C-S-C bond vibrations at 1095 and 814 cm−1 due to PTBr –GQD interaction [23]. On additional note, the C = O stretching vibration of graphene quantum dot (GQD) at 1661 cm−1 showed a variable peak intensity; it was higher in the ex-situ composite than in the in-situ one, perhaps due to relatively high concentration of graphene quantum dot (GQD) in the sample [17]. Compared with pristine poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr), C-Br stretching vibration appeared sharper in the composite due to a probable interaction with graphene quantum dot (GQD) [24]. The Ultraviolet-visible absorption spectra of pristine poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr), graphene quantum dot (GQD) and their composites are shown in Fig. 1b. Graphene quantum dot (GQD) originally showed two characteristic absorption peaks, one at 237 nm owing to п-п* transition of aromatic C = C and the other one at 307 nm for n-п* transition of C = O [25]. In Poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr), a broad absorption peak was noted at 413 nm corresponding to п-п* transition (Fig. 1b). The composites, however, had shown two absorption peaks of variable intensity at two widely different positions from that in the pristine graphene quantum dot (GQD). In ex-situ composite, the first absorption peak had appeared at 280 nm and the second one at 394 nm with diminished peak intensity than the preceding one. The larger peak at 280 nm probably denoted the red shifted п-п* transition of graphene quantum dot (GQD) while the smaller one at 394 nm was attributed to the blue shifted п-п* transition of poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr). In in-situ composite, both these peaks were red shifted and appeared at 283 and 409 nm, respectively with the latter being more intensified than the former. It was apparently seemed that the original absorption peak corresponding to п-п* transition in graphene quantum dot (GQD) shifted in both the composites because of the interaction between poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) and graphene quantum dot (GQD) and thereby increased the net conjugation length [26]. The hyperchromic effect observed in ex-situ composite (n-п* transition) was due to higher relative concentration of graphene quantum dot (GQD) as anticipated during infrared spectroscopic analysis; in the in-situ composite, however, it occurred because of higher concentration of poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) along with relatively strong PTBr-GQD interaction.

Fig. 2 shows the visual appearance of poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) and its composites in suspension. Pristine poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr), was blackish under normal light while appeared light green under UV A. The in-situ composite, on the other hand, looked deep orange under normal light but appeared deep green under UV A while the ex-situ composite which normally looked light orange, appeared bluish green under UV A, respectively.

Scanning electron microscopic (SEM) images of ex-situ and in-situ composites and their respective elemental compositions are shown in Fig. 3 a. The ex-situ composite displayed a regular, fibrillar morphology wherein the thin poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) nanorods grew vertically on the graphene quantum dot (GQD) surface. On the other hand, the in-situ composite displayed an irregular, particulate morphology with an ‘over growth’ of poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) on graphene quantum dot (GQD) surface. The Energy dispersive X-Ray (EDX) data showed a higher C:Br and C:S mass ratios in the in-situ sample which eventually proved the higher mass concentration of poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) per PTBr-GQD hybrid moiety in the in-situ composite than in the ex-situ one. It was further in line with the quantitative estimation of the organic to inorganic mass ratio obtained from ash content measurement under nitrogen whereby the in-situ composite showed an organic to inorganic mass ratio of 1.12:1 while the ex-situ composite showed the mass ratio of 0.98:1, respectively.

The transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images in Fig. 3b further revealed a regular and uniform array of poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) nanorods on graphene quantum dot (GQD) surface in the ex-situ composite. The average thickness of the nanorod was 8 nm. In the in-situ composite, the features were non-uniform due to the over growth of poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr). The average thickness in this case rose to 12 nm. It may be noted that, good quality images at similar magnification could not be captured due to difficult sample texture.

The transmission electron microscopic (TEM) image analysis data (Software: Image J, version 1.45) displayed in Fig. 4 . revealed an interesting fact. The ex-situ composite was found to possess a wider distribution in both length and area of the hybrid moieties than in the in-situ one. The length of most of the moieties in the in-situ composite was 0.02 mm (2 × 103 nm) while it was 10 nm only in the ex-situ sample. Similarly, the hybrid moieties were much wider in the in-situ sample (maximum moieties possessed an area of 1000 sq. nm) as compared to that (200 sq. nm area for most of the moieties) in the ex-situ case.

The crystallographic pattern of ex-situ and in-situ composites and the reference samples are shown in Fig. 5 . The diffraction pattern of neat poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) was wide, indicated its dominant amorphous character (low percentage crystallinity of 26%). It showed a reflection peak at 2θ=26° corresponding to the reflection from (002) plane. In the in-situ composite, the reflection angle was reduced to 24.8° while in the ex-situ sample it was further reduced to 23.4° Graphene oxide (GO) originally showed two characteristic reflection peaks –one at 26.05° with lower intensity corresponding to the reflection from (111) and the other one at 11.54° because of the reflection from (002), respectively [17]. The reflection from (111) probably merged with that from (002) of poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) in its composites and further shifted to a lower reflection angle because of strong intercalative diffusion of poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) molecules inside the stacking gallery of GO during the polymerization stage. The other characteristic reflection of graphene oxide (GO) at 11.54° was weakly visible in both the composites because of its lower concentration than the pristine form. However, in spite of that, it was still more visible (higher intensity) in the ex-situ composite than in the in-situ one, due to its higher relative concentration. The overall crystallinity of the in-situ composite was low (25%) alike pristine poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) (26%), while it was slightly higher (30%) in the ex-situ sample probably due to the seeded graphene quantum dot (GQD) which aided effective crystallization and growth of the poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) molecules [19].

Both ex-situ and in-situ composites were photo-excited at 390 nm and the emission behavior was recorded and displayed in Fig. 6 a. The in-situ composite exhibited a distinct emission peak at 540 nm. As compared with pristine poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr), the concerned peak was highly de-intensified and red shifted (516 nm to 540 nm) because of the strong PTBr-GQD interaction along with possible transition effects of oxygen rich functional groups in graphene quantum dot (GQD) which further promoted the п-п* electronic transition [2].

Unlike in-situ composite, the emission peak of the ex- situ composite did not register any shift and appeared at the similar position as that of the pristine poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) as a mark of weaker interfacial interaction. Along with, two additional factors like і) surface passivation of graphene quantum dot (GQD) through effective adsorption and subsequent charge transfer from poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) [27] and ii) distortion of sp2 domain due to partial changes that occurred in the chemical structure during synthesis [28], respectively, were also responsible as well. The emission intensity, however, was found to be only slightly reduced compared to that in the in-situ composite. The stronger quenching, on the other hand, as exhibited by the in-situ composite was mainly due to low graphene quantum dot (GQD) concentration in the latter. This could well be treated as a manifestation of confinement effect as described by Yang et al. [29] whereby due to strong interfacial interaction the molecules of poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) functioned as a ligand and absorbed the photo-energy and transferred it to graphene quantum dot (GQD), wherein the latter was functioning as an acceptor [30]. Despite low graphene quantum dot (GQD) concentration, stronger interface facilitated efficient electron transfer between poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) and graphene quantum dot (GQD) prior to the electron-hole recombination occurred in the in-situ composite [31].

The excited state fluorescence decay curves of the composites are shown in Fig. 6b. The decay curve for the in-situ composite was fitted well in a bi-exponential pattern with prevalence of two chemical species in the excited state whereas the ex-situ composite could only be fitted in to a multi-exponential decay pattern with prevalence of at least three different chemical species in the excited state. Life times of individual species along with the average life time calculated from the data are reported in Table 1 . The average life time of the in-situ composite was higher with lower relative abundance than the ex-situ sample, which indeed, nominated the in-situ composite as a better fluorescent material amicable for display application [32].

Fig. 7 . and 8
represent a typical linear sweep voltametric plot of PTBr/GQD composites in dark and on illumination (Fig. 7a. for in-situ and Fig. 8a. for ex-situ, respectively). The photocurrent efficiency of these materials was evaluated by a parameter called photosensitivity (P), as showed in Eq. (2).
(2)
P =
J L
−
J D
J D

The
J L
and
J D
were the photocurrent density measured in light and dark respectively [33]. We had used 105 Hz as high frequency and 102 Hz as low frequency in the experiment. The AC amplitude used was 5 mV or 0.005 V. The composite loading on the electrodes in the electrochemical experiments was 0.45 μg cm−2 for the in-situ sample and 0.65 μg cm−2 for the ex-situ sample, respectively. The value of P for both the composites was calculated and reported in Table 3. The data revealed that the in-situ sample produced a higher magnitude of photosensitivity compared to the ex-situ one despite of its lower concentration on the electrode thus indicating its favorable photoconductivity response. On the contrary, the ex-situ sample showed an almost negligible photosensitivity as a result of weaker interaction between poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) and graphene quantum dot (GQD). At this point it is imperative to mention about the photosensitivities of poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) and grapheme quantum dot (GQD)- the former yielded a photocurrent efficiency of 0.06 in dark while the latter produced the photocurrent of 0.008 mA [34], respectively, both much lower than the in-situ (1.436 mA) and the ex-situ (0.115 mA) composites. Synergism was thus achieved through composite formation with much better result achieved in the in-situ route due to stronger PTBr-GQD interfacing. The result was even better than some of the other new-age composites of the likes of polyaniline/GO [35], poly (9, 9′-dioctyl-fluorene-co-bithiophene)/ RGO [36] etc. developed so far. The polyaniline/GO had yielded a photocurrent efficiency of only 0.0091 mA as compared to 0.115 mA exhibited by the ex-situ composite.

The photocurrent-time response curve of the in-situ and the ex-situ composites are displayed in Fig. 7b. and 8b, respectively. The consecutive rise and fall of the current density due to continuous light on and off at a fixed interval indicated a clear photo-responsive nature of both the samples. However, it was also clear from Fig. 7b. that the in-situ composite showed a more stable photocurrent response till a time period of 250 s whereas the ex-situ composite showed a steady drop (Fig. 8b.) in the same time interval because of poor photostability compared to that of the in-situ one.

The electrochemical impedance characteristics of the composites were investigated to study their charge transfer behavior. Fig. 7c. and 8c display the typical Nyquist plot of the in-situ and ex-situ composites, which consisted of a characteristic semicircle spread between the high and the low frequency regions. The high and the low frequency limits in a Nyquist plot are the characteristic of solution resistance (Rs) and total resistance (Rs + Rct), respectively. In order to identify the resistive parameters of the composites, the collected impedance data were fitted in the equivalent circuit model displayed in the inset of Fig. 7c. The model contains a solution resistance (Rs), charge transfer resistance (Rct) and a capacitance combined with Warburg element (Zw) and constant phase element, respectively [37]. The resistive parameters obtained from the above circuit model are reported in Table 2
. The value of Rct which signifies the charge transfer resistance between electrolytic solution and the electrode layer containing the composites were exceptionally low for the in-situ sample compared to the ex-situ one, indicating an effective electron transfer at the interface. In addition to that, the shorter arc radius obtained under light compared to the dark in Fig. 7c. for the in-situ composite further confirmed its improved nature of interfacial electron transfer under illumination and therefore justified as a superior photoconductive material. The capacitance values, also obtained from the equivalent circuit fitting model, are summarized in Table 2 (expressed in μF μg − 1 cm2) [38]. The in-situ composite clearly possessed 6 to 7 times higher capacitance value compared to the ex-situ one (in both dark and light) which served as an additional indicator of better charge holding capacity of the in-situ sample and further established it as a superior electrochemical material. A slight enhancement of capacitance value under illumination than dark in both the cases, however, was attributed to the increase of space charge. On illumination, a greater number of charge carriers were generated and thereby increased the total space charge which in turn affected the capacitative ability of the composites and the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy was normalized along with the composite loading [39].

Both experimental data and images indicated successful development of PTBr/GQD composites following the facile in-situ and ex-situ routes but with a characteristic difference. Although both the methods produced hybrid moieties composed of seeded graphene quantum dot (GQD) inside the viscoelastic poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr), but contained a higher proportion of poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr), in the composite formed via in-situ route largely because of the effective diffusion of monomer molecules inside the GO gallery before polymerization. The hybrid moieties in the in-situ composite thus appeared much larger and more irregular in shape but with a narrower variation in overall shape and size, as compared to the ex-situ one, where the finer moieties yielded a much wider variation in their shape and size in the latter. The data further revealed that the overgrowth of the polymer was instrumental in increasing its conjugation length and resulted into more interactive interface than the ex-situ composite and facilitated effective electron transfer between poly (3-bromothiophene) (PTBr) and graphene quantum dot (GQD). Although the latter facilitated a stronger red shift but, at the same time, exhibited a stronger quenching than the ex-situ composite because of faster electron transfer than the electron-hole recombination. However, such quenched systems with strong red shift are still useful as light emitting diode (LED), solar cell and in bioimaging as the applied electric field can increase the charge separation and slow down the electron recombination. Both the composites, on the other hand, exhibited stronger photocurrent efficiency than the pristine components and showed synergism. Stronger interfacial interaction leading to efficient electron transfer, however, aided higher photocurrent efficiency with higher stability in the in-situ composite compared to that in the ex-situ one.
Soumen Sardar- Synthesized the material, characterized the product, wrote the article and monitored the whole work.
Indranil Roy- Synthesized and characterized GO
Subhadeep Chakraborty- Investigated, did formal analysis.
Abhisek Brata Ghosh- Did the photoelectrochemical investigation.
Abhijit Bandyopadhyay- Conceptualized the work, framed the methodology, curated the data, administered the project and provided overall guidance.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors, Soumen Sardar and Abhisek Brata Ghosh, both thankfully acknowledge University Grant Commission (UGC), Government of India for providing financial assistance under UGC-JRF scheme (Ref. No. 22/12/2013(ii)EU-V) and Dr. D. S. Kothari Post-Doctoral Fellowship scheme (ID: 201819-CH/19-20/0012), respectively, for carrying out this project work.
